title: Putin-Xi Summit Follows Trump Visit as Great Power Competition Intensifies
subhead: Just days after Trump's high-stakes Beijing summit, Putin arrives for talks with Xi as Russia strikes Chinese shipping near Ukraine and Ukraine launches its largest drone offensive of the war. Meanwhile, France pivots toward East Africa and G7 ministers coordinate sanctions strategy in Paris.

sections.heading: Putin-Xi Summit Tests Beijing's Balancing Act
sections.body:
Vladimir Putin arrived in Beijing on Monday for talks with Xi Jinping, just four days after Donald Trump concluded a two-day state visit to China. The timing is deliberate: Xi is managing parallel relationships with Washington and Moscow while both relationships face strain from different directions.

The Chinese readout from the Trump summit introduced a new framework — "constructive strategic stability" — with Xi warning that Taiwan remains the issue most likely to trigger conflict between the two powers. Xi stated bluntly that "Taiwan independence and cross-Strait peace are as irreconcilable as fire and water." Trump declined to commit to continued arms sales to Taiwan during the visit.

Putin's visit comes amid growing friction: Russia struck a Chinese cargo vessel bound for Ukraine on Monday, damaging the ship and sparking a fire. The KSL DEYANG, flying under a Marshall Islands flag, was heading to Ukraine's Pivdennyi port to load iron ore when it was hit by a Russian drone. Zelensky noted that "the Russians could not have been unaware of what vessel was at sea."
sections.whyItMatters: Beijing is trying to maintain partnerships with both Washington and Moscow while avoiding direct confrontation with either. Striking Chinese shipping tests that balance — and creates awkward optics just as Putin seeks Xi's continued support.

sections.heading: Ukraine Launches Largest Drone Offensive of the War
sections.body:
Russia intercepted 3,124 Ukrainian drones in the past week, including 1,054 destroyed on May 17 alone — one of the largest aerial assaults of the war. The scale represents a significant Ukrainian manufacturing and operational capacity that has survived Russian strikes on energy and defense infrastructure.

Russia responded with a massive overnight barrage of its own, launching 524 drones and 22 missiles at Ukraine. The main target was Dnipro, where dozens were injured and residential buildings were damaged. Air defense units shot down or neutralized 503 drones and four missiles.

The drone war has intensified as both sides target infrastructure. Russian forces struck Naftogaz energy facilities in the Dnipropetrovsk region, destroying a filling station and injuring two employees. Meanwhile, Ukrainian forces continue to pressure Russian border regions, with two killed in Belgorod from Ukrainian drone attacks.
sections.whyItMatters: The aerial war has entered a new phase of mutual bombardment. Ukraine is demonstrating it can sustain large-scale drone production despite Russian strikes, while Russia shows it can still overwhelm Ukrainian air defenses in certain sectors.

sections.heading: G7 Ministers Coordinate Ukraine Support in Paris
sections.body:
Finance ministers from the G7 met in Paris on Monday to discuss ongoing support for Ukraine amid the intensified conflict. Ukraine's Finance Minister Serhii Marchenko called for increased sanctions pressure on Russia, while confirming that the first disbursement from the previously agreed 90 billion euro EU loan is expected in early June.

European Economic Commissioner Valdis Dombrovskis reaffirmed the need to open the Strait of Hormuz, linking the Iran conflict to broader economic concerns. The dual focus on Ukraine and Iran reflects how the two conflicts are increasingly interconnected in Western strategic planning.

The EU also announced new sanctions on May 11 against 16 individuals and seven entities responsible for the systematic unlawful deportation of Ukrainian children to Russia. The measures are part of an ongoing sanctions regime that continues to expand despite concerns about enforcement gaps.
sections.whyItMatters: Western financial and sanctions coordination continues even as attention splits between Ukraine and Iran. The 90 billion euro loan disbursement in June will be a critical test of whether EU support mechanisms remain functional at scale.

sections.heading: France Pivots to East Africa with Kenya Summit
sections.body:
French President Emmanuel Macron arrived in Nairobi on Sunday ahead of the Africa Forward Summit, marking France's attempt to rebrand its relationship with the continent from post-colonial dominance to "partnership of equals." The summit, held for the first time in an Anglophone country, draws 30 heads of state and follows the complete withdrawal of French troops from West Africa last year.

Kenya and France signed 11 agreements covering nuclear energy, transport modernization, and sustainable agriculture. Kenya's President William Ruto emphasized that Nairobi is "neither looking East nor West" but "looking forward," signaling continued African preference for non-aligned partnerships.

Kenya's opposition leader Kalonzo Musyoka criticized the summit's timing, citing concerns about democracy and human rights ahead of the 2027 general election. The criticism highlights the domestic political constraints facing African leaders as they engage with former colonial powers.
sections.whyItMatters: France is attempting to rebuild African influence after losing its military foothold in the Sahel. The pivot to East Africa and Anglophone partners represents a strategic shift, but faces competition from other powers and domestic political resistance in host countries.

sections.heading: Iran Tensions Simmer as Hormuz Crisis Enters Third Month
sections.body:
The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed to tanker traffic as the US-Iran standoff continues with no clear resolution timeline. The closure has pushed oil prices higher and forced major economies to draw down strategic reserves while exploring alternative supply routes.

CNN reported that Iran is developing capabilities beneath the Strait, though details remain limited. The report suggests Tehran is positioning for a prolonged confrontation rather than seeking a quick diplomatic off-ramp.

The conflict was a central topic during Trump's Beijing summit, with both the US and China seeking to manage the economic fallout. China remains a major purchaser of Iranian oil and has interests in seeing the strait reopened, while also opposing American military presence in the region.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz closure is now a structural feature of global energy markets rather than a temporary disruption. The longer it persists, the more pressure builds on all sides for either escalation or negotiation.

sections.heading: Hungary's Political Shift Reshapes EU Dynamics
sections.body:
Viktor Orbán's defeat in Hungary's April elections has removed Russia's most reliable advocate inside the European Union. The new TISZA government holds a supermajority and has signaled intent to align more closely with EU positions on Ukraine and sanctions.

The shift matters for sanctions enforcement and EU enlargement talks. Orbán's government had served as a brake on multiple EU initiatives, from aid packages to Ukraine to the expansion of sanctions regimes. His removal changes the calculus for EU decision-making on Russia policy.

While the transition is ongoing, early signals suggest the new government will not exercise the vetoes that characterized the Orbán era. This could accelerate EU support for Ukraine and streamline decision-making on security issues.
sections.whyItMatters: Moscow lost its most effective advocate inside the EU. The change removes a systematic obstacle to European coordination on Ukraine and sanctions, though the new government's full policy direction remains to be seen.

bottomLine: Three great power summits in one week — Trump-Xi, then Putin-Xi — show how Beijing sits at the center of current geopolitical dynamics. China is managing relationships with both Washington and Moscow while trying to avoid entanglement in either conflict. The strike on Chinese shipping near Ukraine complicates that balancing act. Meanwhile, the Ukraine war has entered a new phase of mutual drone bombardment, and France is attempting to rebuild African influence after its Sahel retreat. The Hormuz closure continues to shape global energy markets with no resolution in sight.
